title: Schedules and the Delta Conjecture
overall_summary: The paper develops schedule formulas for weighted enumerations of parking functions and valley-marked parking functions, with the marked version giving an explicit product formula for the combinatorial side of the Delta Conjecture after taking Hilbert-series specializations. Its core technique is an insertion tree: build parking functions one car at a time so that area changes deterministically and dinv changes through a full q-integer, producing factors indexed by schedule numbers. It also proposes a monomial basis for the super-diagonal coinvariant ring SDR_n inspired by the Carlsson-Oblomkov basis for diagonal coinvariants. For the original problem, the most directly relevant ingredient is the recalled Schröder-path formula for hook Schur coefficients of ∇e_n, together with the paper's method of isolating hook coefficients via tensoring with an exterior, Grassmann algebra and taking the sign-isotypic component. The schedule and ordered-set-partition machinery is useful as a template for converting symmetric-function/operator coefficients into explicit combinatorial counts.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1, Shuffle Theorem
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n ≥ 1, let PF_n be the set of parking functions of size n with labels [n]. For a parking function PF, area(PF) is the number of cells between its Dyck path and the main diagonal, dinv(PF) is the number of diagonal inversion pairs, and ides(PF) is the inverse descent set of its diagonal-reading word. Let F_S denote Gessel's fundamental quasisymmetric function indexed by a subset S of {1,...,n−1}. Let ∇ be the Macdonald eigenoperator defined on modified Macdonald polynomials by ∇ H̃_μ(X;q,t) = t^{n(μ)} q^{n(μ')} H̃_μ(X;q,t), where n(μ)=∑_i (i−1) μ_i and μ' is the conjugate partition. Then ∇ e_n = ∑_{PF in PF_n} t^{area(PF)} q^{dinv(PF)} F_{ides(PF)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this theorem as the Carlsson-Mellit proof of the Shuffle Conjecture and does not reprove it. The definitions of the statistics are included because the later schedule formulas refine the right-hand side. The theorem supplies the bridge from the operator ∇e_n to parking-function enumeration; taking an appropriate Hilbert-series specialization later removes the fundamental quasisymmetric functions and leaves the bivariate area-dinv generating function.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2, Haiman's diagonal coinvariant theorem
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n ≥ 1, let DR_n be the diagonal coinvariant ring C[x_1,...,x_n,y_1,...,y_n] / I_n(X,Y), where I_n(X,Y) is generated by all nonconstant S_n-invariant polynomials under the diagonal action permuting x_i and y_i simultaneously. With q tracking x-degree and t tracking y-degree, the Frobenius characteristic of DR_n is Frob(DR_n;q,t) = ∇ e_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Haiman's theorem and does not reprove it. This result identifies the representation-theoretic object DR_n with the symmetric-function side of the Shuffle Theorem. Combining it with Theorem 2.1 gives parking-function formulas for the Frobenius characteristic and, after taking the scalar product with h_1^n, for the Hilbert series.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.1, Hilbert series of diagonal coinvariants by parking functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n ≥ 1, Hilb(DR_n;q,t) = ⟨∇e_n, h_1^n⟩ = ∑_{PF in PF_n} t^{area(PF)} q^{dinv(PF)}, where h_1^n is the n-th power of the first complete homogeneous symmetric function and PF_n is the set of parking functions of size n labeled by [n].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For any S_n-module M, the scalar product ⟨Frob(M), h_1^n⟩ gives the Hilbert series, because the coefficient of the monomial symmetric function m_{1^n} in a Schur function s_λ equals the dimension of the irreducible S_n-module indexed by λ. Applying this to Haiman's identity Frob(DR_n)=∇e_n gives the first equality. Applying the Shuffle Theorem then sums over all parking functions; in this Hilbert specialization each fundamental quasisymmetric function contributes one.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4, uniqueness of unmarked insertion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let PF be a nonempty parking function. In the paper's unmarked insertion algorithm, a new car c is inserted into diagonal k of a smaller parking function PF' under the hypotheses that the (k+1)-diagonal of PF' is empty and the k-diagonal contains no car smaller than c; possible insertion sites are determined by cars s<c in diagonal k−1, cars b>c in diagonal k, and additionally the lower-left corner when k=0. Then for every nonempty PF there is exactly one triple (PF', c, k) such that PF is obtained from PF' by this insertion algorithm. Namely, k is the highest nonempty diagonal of PF, c is the smallest car in that diagonal, and PF' is obtained by deleting c and shifting all cars in higher rows down and left once.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The highest nonempty diagonal of a parking function must contain the last inserted car, because the insertion algorithm only inserts into a diagonal above which there are no cars. Within that diagonal, the algorithm requires that no smaller car already lie in the target diagonal, so the inserted car is forced to be the smallest car in the highest diagonal. Deleting that car and reversing the row shift recovers the unique predecessor. The construction also checks that the predecessor satisfies the hypotheses needed for insertion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5, q-integer weight change under unmarked insertion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let PF be a parking function, let c be a car not in PF, and let k be a diagonal index for which the unmarked insertion algorithm applies. Then ∑_{PF' in Insert(PF,c,k)} t^{area(PF')} q^{dinv(PF')} = t^{area(PF)+k} q^{dinv(PF)} [|Insert(PF,c,k)|]_q, where [r]_q = 1+q+...+q^{r−1} and Insert(PF,c,k) is the set of all parking functions obtained by inserting c into diagonal k of PF.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Every insertion of c into diagonal k increases area by exactly k. The relative order and diagonals of all old cars are unchanged, so old dinv pairs remain and no new dinv pairs can occur except those involving c. The possible insertion sites are exactly indexed by cars that could form new diagonal inversions with c: cars s<c one diagonal lower for secondary inversions and cars b>c in the same diagonal for primary inversions. At the rightmost site c creates no new inversion; each step moving c one possible site to the left crosses exactly one such car and adds exactly one new dinv. Thus the dinv values are dinv(PF), dinv(PF)+1, ..., dinv(PF)+|Insert(PF,c,k)|−1, giving the q-integer factor.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.6, Haglund-Loehr schedule formula for parking functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let τ be a permutation of length n, decomposed into increasing runs, and append a 0 to the end of τ. For a car c in τ, define its schedule number w_τ(c) as the number of cars larger than c in the same run plus the number of cars smaller than c in the following run; the appended 0 accounts for the extra insertion point in diagonal 0. Let PF(τ) be the set of parking functions whose schedule is τ, meaning that the cars in each diagonal are listed in increasing order and the diagonals are read from highest to lowest to form τ. Then ∑_{PF in PF(τ)} t^{area(PF)} q^{dinv(PF)} = t^{maj(τ)} ∏_{c in τ} [w_τ(c)]_q, where maj(τ) is the sum of the descent positions of τ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Build an insertion tree from τ by starting with the last car of τ and inserting the remaining cars from right to left. If a car lies in the (k+1)-st run from the end of τ, insert it into diagonal k. Proposition 3.4 implies that every parking function of schedule τ appears exactly once as a leaf of this tree. At each insertion, Lemma 3.5 multiplies the generating function by t^k times a q-integer whose length is exactly the schedule number w_τ(c), because the insertion sites are counted by larger cars in the same run and smaller cars in the next run. Summing the diagonal indices k over all inserted cars gives maj(τ), yielding the displayed product.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.8, q-integer weight change under marked insertion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let MPF be a valley-marked parking function, let c be a car not in MPF, and let k be a diagonal index for which the marked insertion algorithm applies. In this algorithm c is inserted as a marked car into diagonal k, with possible sites determined by unmarked cars s<c in diagonal k and unmarked cars b>c in diagonal k+1. Then ∑_{MPF' in Insert*(MPF,c,k)} t^{area(MPF')} q^{dinv(MPF')} = t^{area(MPF)+k} q^{dinv(MPF)} [|Insert*(MPF,c,k)|]_q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: As in the unmarked case, inserting c into diagonal k increases area by k and preserves all old relative positions and old dinv pairs. The new marked car c can only create diagonal inversions with unmarked eligible cars, and the algorithm is designed so that the eligible cars are exactly the possible new inversion partners. Unlike unmarked insertion, c creates an inversion with the insertion-site car and with all eligible sites to its left; however the definition of marked dinv subtracts one for the newly marked valley. Thus, as c is placed from left to right through the possible sites, the net dinv increments are 0,1,...,|Insert*(MPF,c,k)|−1. This again gives a q-integer factor.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.9, uniqueness of marked insertion under the prescribed order
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let MPF be a valley-marked parking function containing at least one marked car. Among triples (MPF', c, k) such that MPF is obtained by marked insertion of c into diagonal k of MPF' and such that no diagonal above k contains a marked car, there is exactly one. Specifically, k is the highest diagonal of MPF containing a marked car, c is the largest marked car in that diagonal, and MPF' is obtained by deleting c and shifting all higher cars down and left once.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Marked cars may be inserted in different diagonal orders unless an order is imposed. The paper imposes the order from lower to higher diagonals and, within a diagonal, from larger to smaller cars when reversing. Therefore the last marked insertion must be the largest marked car in the highest diagonal containing any marked cars. Deleting this car and undoing the row shift gives the unique predecessor; the column-increasing condition is preserved because if c lay beneath a car b and had a left neighbor s in its diagonal, then s<c<b.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.15, insertability criterion for marked permutations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A marked permutation τ* is insertable by the paper's process, meaning that its unmarked cars can be inserted first from right to left by the unmarked algorithm and then its marked cars can be inserted from right to left by the marked algorithm to produce at least one marked parking function, if and only if τ* does not begin with a marked car and the unmarked permutation hatτ obtained by deleting all marked cars from τ* has the same number of increasing runs as τ*.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If τ* begins with a marked car, then when that car is to be inserted there is neither a smaller unmarked car in the same diagonal nor any car in the diagonal above, so no marked insertion site exists. If deleting marked cars reduces the number of runs, then either an entire run consisted of marked cars, causing the unmarked insertion process to skip a diagonal before trying to insert into a higher one, or two adjacent unmarked cars in hatτ came from different runs of τ*, forcing an insertion into a diagonal with no eligible supporting car; both are impossible. Conversely, if τ* starts unmarked and run count is preserved, the unmarked insertion tree for hatτ is valid by the unmarked theory. For each later marked insertion of a car c into diagonal k, if diagonal k+1 is nonempty then its leftmost car either is larger than c and supplies a site or is smaller than c and has a smaller car below it supplying a site; if diagonal k+1 is empty, then c belongs to the first run and the initial unmarked car gives a smaller unmarked car in diagonal k. Hence every marked insertion can be performed.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.11, insertable marked permutations are ordered-set-partition schedules
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For an ordered set partition Π of a finite set S of positive integers, define τ(Π) recursively as follows. If Π has one block, list that block increasingly. Otherwise remove the first block B, compute τ(Π') for the remaining ordered set partition Π', let r be the first element of τ(Π'), and form τ(Π) by listing the elements of B greater than r increasingly, then the elements of B less than r increasingly, then τ(Π'). Define τ*(Π) by marking every entry that is not the leftmost element of its block in this word. Then a marked permutation is insertable if and only if it equals τ*(Π) for some ordered set partition Π.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a word τ*(Π), the leftmost element of each block is unmarked, so the word does not start with a marked car. Contiguous strings of marked cars come from one block and are attached to a neighboring unmarked element in the same run, so no entire run is marked. The recursive ordering of each block ensures that any descent involving a marked car persists after deleting the marked cars; hence deleting marked entries does not reduce the number of runs. Lemma 3.15 then gives insertability. Conversely, for an insertable marked permutation τ*, insert bars before each unmarked element and erase markings to form an ordered set partition Π. If τ* differed from the recursively defined τ*(Π), choose the rightmost block where it differs. Any other ordering in that block creates an unnecessary descent that disappears after deleting marked cars, contradicting the run-preservation condition in Lemma 3.15.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.13, schedule formula for valley-marked parking functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Π be an ordered set partition, and let τ*(Π) be its marked permutation schedule as in Theorem 3.11. Let MPF(Π) be the set of valley-marked parking functions of type τ*(Π), meaning that the cars in each diagonal, with their markings, match the runs of τ*(Π). Define schedule numbers w_Π(c) as follows after appending a 0 to τ*(Π): if c is unmarked, w_Π(c) is the number of unmarked cars larger than c in the same run plus the number of unmarked cars smaller than c in the next run; if c is marked, w_Π(c) is the number of unmarked cars smaller than c in the same run plus the number of unmarked cars larger than c in the previous run. Then ∑_{MPF in MPF(Π)} t^{area(MPF)} q^{dinv(MPF)} = t^{maj(τ(Π))} ∏_c [w_Π(c)]_q, where the product is over all cars c in Π.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The insertion tree for τ*(Π) first inserts all unmarked cars from right to left using the unmarked algorithm and then inserts all marked cars from right to left using the marked algorithm. Theorem 3.11 guarantees that exactly the marked permutations arising from ordered set partitions are insertable, and Propositions 3.4 and 3.9 give uniqueness of the reverse insertion process, so the leaves are exactly MPF(Π). Lemmas 3.5 and 3.8 show that each insertion contributes a factor t^k [r]_q, where r is the number of legal insertion sites. The definition of w_Π(c) records precisely these legal-site counts for unmarked and marked cars. Summing all diagonal indices k over the insertion sequence gives maj(τ(Π)), which is the minimaj statistic of the ordered set partition in the Delta Conjecture literature.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1, Carlsson-Oblomkov monomial basis for DR_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For τ in S_n, define Y_τ = ∏_{i: τ_i>τ_{i+1}} y_{τ_1} y_{τ_2} ... y_{τ_i}, the product over descents of τ of the initial y-variable string up to that descent. Define X_τ = ∏_{i=1}^{n−1} (1 + x_{τ_i} + x_{τ_i}^2 + ... + x_{τ_i}^{w_τ(τ_i)−1}), where w_τ is the schedule number from Theorem 3.6. Then a monomial basis for the diagonal coinvariant ring DR_n is obtained by taking the union over all τ in S_n of all monomials occurring in Y_τ X_τ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this as Carlsson and Oblomkov's theorem and does not prove it. The relevance in this paper is that, after setting all x_i equal to q and all y_i equal to t, the number and degrees of monomials in Y_τ X_τ reproduce the schedule product t^{maj(τ)} ∏_c [w_τ(c)]_q of Theorem 3.6. This basis motivates the paper's candidate super-diagonal basis, where ordered set partitions and θ-variables replace ordinary permutations and unmarked schedules.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.7, degree inequality for marked parking functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If MPF is a valley-marked parking function of size n with k marked valleys, then area(MPF) + dinv(MPF) + binom(k+1,2) ≤ binom(n,2). Here dinv(MPF) is the number of primary and secondary diagonal inversion pairs allowed by the markings, minus the number k of marked valleys.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof injects the objects counted by dinv and by pairs of marked cars into cells above the Dyck path. For each diagonal inversion pair (c_1,c_2), with c_1 in a column strictly left of c_2, follow c_1's diagonal to the right until the first East step between the two cars that returns to that diagonal, and take the cell in that East step's column and c_2's row; this map φ is injective because one can recover c_1 by tracing diagonally left from the East step and recover c_2 by tracing right. Since dinv subtracts k, the number of actual inversion pairs is dinv(MPF)+k. For each pair of marked cars c_1 left of c_2, define a second cell ψ(c_1,c_2): if c_1 is in a weakly higher diagonal than c_2 use the same construction as φ, while if c_1 is lower use the East step immediately preceding c_1 and the row of c_2. This is also injective, has image size binom(k,2), and its image is disjoint from φ because marked cars cannot serve as the relevant unmarked left car in a diagonal inversion. The area cells, the φ-image, and the ψ-image are disjoint cells above the main diagonal, whose total number is binom(n,2), giving area + dinv + k + binom(k,2) ≤ binom(n,2).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.8, realization of all allowed marked parking function degrees
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n ≥ 1 and let a,b,c be nonnegative integers satisfying a + b + binom(c+1,2) ≤ binom(n,2). Then there exists a valley-marked parking function MPF of size n with area(MPF)=a, dinv(MPF)=b, and exactly c marked valleys.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write a uniquely in the form a=(n−1)+(n−2)+...+(n−k)+r with 0≤k<n and 0≤r<n−k−1. Construct the Dyck path D(a)=N^k (NE)^r (EN)^{n−k−r} E^k, which has area a. Label this path uniquely so that its diagonal-reading word is n,n−1,...,1. The inequality implies that there are enough cells above the path to ensure at least c+1 cars in the highest two diagonals; mark the rightmost c of these cars to obtain a marked parking function MPF(a,c). The injection argument of Theorem 4.7 is tight for this construction, so its dinv is the maximum allowed by the inequality. Let Π(a,c) be its schedule. The product formula of Theorem 3.13 for MPF(Π(a,c)) has q-support covering all dinv values from 0 up to that maximum, so one can choose a marked parking function with the same area and number of marks and with any desired b in the allowed range.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.9, degree support of the candidate basis B_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B_n be the paper's candidate monomial set for SDR_n: for each ordered set partition Π of [n], form Y_Π from descents of τ(Π), form Θ_Π as the ordered product of θ_i over the marked cars in τ*(Π), and form X_Π=∏_{i=1}^n (1+x_i+...+x_i^{w_Π(i)−1}); B_n is the union of all monomials in Y_Π Θ_Π X_Π. Then for n ≥ 1 and a,b,c ≥ 0, there is at least one element of B_n with x-degree a, y-degree b, and θ-degree c if and only if a + b + binom(c+1,2) ≤ binom(n,2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Each monomial in B_n corresponds, through the schedule construction, to a marked parking function whose y-degree is its area, whose x-degree is its dinv, and whose θ-degree is its number of marked valleys. Theorem 4.7 gives the necessary inequality for every such monomial. Theorem 4.8 constructs a marked parking function with any triple of degrees satisfying the inequality; applying its schedule gives a monomial in B_n with those degrees.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.10, Frobenius characteristic of the Grassmann quotient G_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G_n be the algebra C[θ_1,...,θ_n]/(θ_1+...+θ_n), where the θ_i are Grassmann variables satisfying θ_i θ_j = −θ_j θ_i and S_n acts by permuting indices. With z tracking total θ-degree, Frob(G_n;z) = ∑_{k=0}^{n−1} z^k s_{n−k,1^k}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The degree-0 part is the trivial representation, giving s_n. The degree-1 part is the quotient of the permutation representation by the invariant line θ_1+...+θ_n, hence is the standard representation V_n with Frobenius characteristic s_{n−1,1}. For 1≤k<n, the degree-k Grassmann component is the exterior power ∧^k V_n. The cited standard representation-theoretic fact is that ∧^k V_n is the Specht module of hook shape (n−k,1^k). Summing these homogeneous pieces gives the formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.13, Schröder-path formula for hook Schur coefficients of ∇e_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the hook range 0≤k≤n−1, the coefficient of the hook Schur function s_{k+1,1^{n−k−1}} in ∇e_n is given by ⟨∇e_n, s_{k+1,1^{n−k−1}}⟩ = ∑_{P in L̃^+_{n,n,k}} t^{area(P)} q^{dinv(P)}. Here L̃^+_{n,n,d} is the set of Schröder paths from (0,0) to (n,n) with n−d North steps, n−d East steps, and d diagonal steps (1,1), staying weakly above the main diagonal and having no diagonal step above the highest North step. The area of a Schröder path is the area after replacing each diagonal step by a North step followed by an East step, and dinv is the number of attacking pairs of North steps, meaning pairs in the same diagonal or in consecutive diagonals with the leftmost North step in the higher diagonal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this result: it was conjectured by Egge, Haglund, Killpatrick, and Kremer and proved by Haglund. It is used in Section 4.2 as the explicit combinatorial interpretation of hook Schur coefficients of ∇e_n. The proof is not reproduced in the paper, but the essential input for later arguments is that every coefficient of t^a q^b in a hook coefficient is counted by Schröder paths with fixed numbers of diagonal steps, area a, and dinv b.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.14, degree inequality for Schröder paths
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any P in L̃^+_{n,n,c+1}, if area(P)=a and dinv(P)=b, then a + b + binom(c+1,2) ≤ binom(n,2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a Schröder-path analogue of the injection used for marked parking functions. For each attacking pair of North steps (N_1,N_2) with N_1 to the left of N_2, follow the diagonal of N_1 to the right until it hits an East step, and assign the cell directly above that East step and directly left of N_2; this gives an injection φ from attacking pairs to cells above the path. For each ordered pair of diagonal steps (D_1,D_2) with D_1 to the left of D_2, assign the cell directly above D_1 and left of D_2; this gives an injection ψ whose image has size binom(c+1,2). The images of φ and ψ are disjoint, and they are disjoint from the cells counted by area. Since all these cells lie above the main diagonal, and there are binom(n,2) such cells, the inequality follows.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.11, sign-isotypic degree bound for DR_n tensor G_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let DR_n be the diagonal coinvariant ring and let G_n be the Grassmann quotient from Lemma 4.10. Give DR_n ⊗ G_n the diagonal S_n-action, with q,t tracking the x,y-degrees from DR_n and z tracking θ-degree from G_n. If the coefficient of the sign Schur function s_{1^n} in Frob(DR_n ⊗ G_n) at degree t^a q^b z^c is nonzero, then a + b + binom(c+1,2) ≤ binom(n,2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Frobenius characteristic of a tensor product with diagonal S_n-action is the Kronecker product of Frobenius characteristics. Therefore sign multiplicities are controlled by Kronecker coefficients g(λ,μ,1^n). Using the dual Cauchy identity, the paper notes that g(λ,μ,1^n)=1 if μ=λ' and 0 otherwise. Lemma 4.10 gives Frob(G_n;z)=∑_{k=0}^{n−1} z^k s_{n−k,1^k}; hence the sign-isotypic generating function is ∑_{k=0}^{n−1} z^k ⟨∇e_n, s_{k+1,1^{n−k−1}}⟩. The hook coefficient is then interpreted by the Schröder-path formula of Theorem 4.13, and the cell-injection estimate of Theorem 4.14 gives the stated inequality for any nonzero coefficient.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.15, sign-isotypic degree bound for SDR_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let SDR_n be the super-diagonal coinvariant ring with commuting x_i,y_i variables and Grassmann θ_i variables, modulo the ideal generated by nonconstant diagonal S_n-invariants. If the coefficient of s_{1^n} in Frob(SDR_n;q,t,z) at degree t^a q^b z^c is nonzero, then a + b + binom(c+1,2) ≤ binom(n,2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper observes that SDR_n is a quotient of DR_n ⊗ G_n under the diagonal S_n-action. A quotient cannot contain a sign-isotypic component in a degree where the larger tensor product has none. Therefore Theorem 4.11 immediately implies the same degree bound for the sign-isotypic part of SDR_n.

proof_techniques.name: Insertion-tree schedule factorization
proof_techniques.description: The paper groups parking functions by a schedule permutation and constructs each group as the set of leaves of an insertion tree. Cars are inserted from right to left according to the runs of the schedule. The key feature is that each insertion has a set of legal sites whose size is a schedule number; area increases by a fixed diagonal index, and dinv takes all consecutive values across those legal sites. Thus a complicated sum over parking functions factors as t^{major index} times a product of q-integers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the hook coefficients c_λ(n) can be expressed as weighted counts of parking-function-like objects, grouping by schedules may turn the coefficient into a finite product formula or a clean combinatorial count. This is especially useful when an operator expression gives a sum with dinv/area statistics but not an immediately transparent coefficient interpretation.

proof_techniques.name: Marked insertion and ordered set partitions
proof_techniques.description: For the Delta Conjecture side, the paper extends insertion schedules to valley-marked parking functions. Marked cars are inserted only after all unmarked cars, and their legal insertion sites are counted by unmarked cars in adjacent diagonals. The insertable marked permutations are characterized exactly as τ*(Π) for ordered set partitions Π, using a recursive minimaj ordering of blocks; this converts a marking problem into ordered-set-partition combinatorics.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook shapes often arise from adding exterior or marked structure, so the ordered-set-partition encoding may provide a useful model if c_λ(n) for hooks is obtained by introducing marks or Grassmann variables. The minimaj ordering gives a canonical way to avoid overcounting while preserving major-index weights.

proof_techniques.name: Extracting hooks via exterior powers and the sign component
proof_techniques.description: The paper introduces the Grassmann quotient G_n whose Frobenius characteristic is a sum of hook Schur functions s_{n−k,1^k}. Tensoring DR_n with G_n and taking the sign-isotypic component uses the Kronecker rule g(λ,μ,1^n)=1 precisely when μ=λ'. This converts sign multiplicities in DR_n ⊗ G_n into hook Schur coefficients of ∇e_n.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a Hilbert series expanded in Schur polynomials in grading variables, an analogous exterior-algebra or sign-extraction device may isolate hook coefficients. The method suggests replacing direct Schur-coefficient extraction by a representation-theoretic construction whose graded dimension is the desired hook coefficient.

proof_techniques.name: Schröder paths for hook Schur coefficients
proof_techniques.description: A cited result of Egge-Haglund-Killpatrick-Kremer and Haglund gives a direct combinatorial formula for ⟨∇e_n, s_{k+1,1^{n−k−1}}⟩ as a q,t-enumeration of Schröder paths with a fixed number of diagonal steps. The statistics are area, defined by replacing diagonal steps with NE, and dinv, counted by attacking pairs of North steps.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem asks specifically for hook-shape coefficients, so this is the closest template in the paper: hook coefficients are counted by Schröder paths with diagonal-step number determined by the hook. A solver should investigate whether the universal coefficients c_λ(n) for hook λ are the same, a specialization, or a higher-m analogue of these Schröder-path counts.

proof_techniques.name: Cell-injection degree bounds
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves inequalities of the form area + dinv + triangular term ≤ binom(n,2) by injecting dinv pairs and pairs of marked or diagonal steps into cells above a Dyck or Schröder path, disjointly from the area cells. For marked parking functions the triangular term comes from pairs of marked cars; for Schröder paths it comes from pairs of diagonal steps.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If hook coefficients have a path interpretation, this injection technique can prove support restrictions and sanity checks for the degrees appearing in c_λ(n). It may also help identify the correct triangular shift attached to a hook's leg or arm length.

proof_techniques.name: Basis-from-schedules heuristic
proof_techniques.description: The Carlsson-Oblomkov basis for DR_n is organized by permutations and schedule numbers: a descent monomial Y_τ records y-degree and a product X_τ records all x-degree choices allowed by schedule numbers. The paper generalizes this pattern conjecturally to SDR_n by replacing permutations with ordered set partitions and adding θ-monomials for marked entries.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For multiset or m-set coinvariant algebras, this suggests searching for a monomial or combinatorial basis indexed by schedules whose multigraded weight generating function is already Schur-positive. Hook coefficients could then be interpreted as the subset of basis elements with exterior or one-row/one-column schedule data.

other_useful_info: Conjecture 2.6 is the valley version of the Delta Conjecture: Δ'_{e_{n-k-1}} e_n equals the weighted sum over valley-marked parking functions with k marks, using area, marked dinv, and ides. Conjecture 2.7, due to Zabrocki, states that Frob(SDR_n;q,t,z)=∑_{k=0}^n z^k Δ'_{e_{n-k-1}} e_n, making SDR_n a proposed representation-theoretic model for the Delta Conjecture. Conjecture 3.14 gives the Hilbert-series consequence predicted by the paper's schedule formula: Hilb(SDR_n;q,t)=∑_Π z^{n−|Π|} t^{maj(τ(Π))} ∏_{c=1}^n [w_Π(c)]_q over ordered set partitions Π of [n]. Conjecture 4.3 proposes the candidate monomial basis B_n for SDR_n constructed from Y_Π, Θ_Π, and X_Π. Conjecture 4.5 predicts that the homogeneous component of SDR_n of x-degree a, y-degree b, and θ-degree c is nonempty if and only if a+b+binom(c+1,2)≤binom(n,2). Remark 4.12 points to Blasiak's combinatorial interpretation of Kronecker coefficients when one shape is a hook, and to work of Egge-Loehr-Warrington and Garsia-Remmel for passing from fundamental quasisymmetric expansions to Schur expansions; these references may be relevant for proving hook coefficient formulas. Remark 4.2 notes that the Carlsson-Oblomkov basis reduces to the Garsia-Stanton basis when all x_i are set to 0 and to the Artin basis when all y_i are set to 0.
why_relevant: Develops schedule formulas for Delta-conjecture Hilbert series and proposes bases for super-diagonal coinvariant rings. These schedule and basis techniques are useful for turning operator expressions into explicit combinatorial counts.
